schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil & Delitzsch identify Hiram from 2 Samuel and the Tyrian archives and frame the whole chapter as temple-preparation paralleling 2 Chronicles 2.
Henry reads the old friendship of Tyre and Israel as godly character winning even those without, and takes Hiram for a worshipper of the true God.
Gill notes the varied Phoenician names for Hiram and reads the embassy as condolence for David and congratulation on Solomon's accession.
Clarke argues that this is the same Hiram who befriended and built for David, not a second king of the name.

debates.question: Is this Hiram the same king who was David's friend, or a later namesake?

sides.position: the same Hiram who loved David and built his palace
some suppose a different, later Hiram

schools.summary: Keil & Delitzsch expound 'to the name of Jehovah' from Deuteronomy 12 as the visible pledge of God's presence, and note David's earlier negotiations with Hiram.
Gill accepts the genuineness of the royal letters, citing Josephus's appeal to the Tyrian archives and the heathen writer Eupolemus.
Clarke reports the same archive tradition but doubts Josephus, holding such claims 'worthy of little credit.'

debates.question: Are the letters between Solomon and Hiram, said to be kept in the Tyrian archives, genuine?

sides.position: genuine, confirmed by Josephus and the heathen Eupolemus
a claim of Josephus worthy of little credit

themes: the temple as pledge of God's presence

schools.summary: Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott ground David's inability in his ceaseless wars and note his earlier gathering of Tyrian cedar (1 Chronicles 22:4).
Gill explains that Hiram knew David's desire both by fame and from personal dealings over cedar-wood.

themes: rest as the condition for building

schools.summary: Keil & Delitzsch identify the 'evil occurrent' with the disasters of David's reign and distinguish the present peace from Hadad's later rising.
Clarke reads the Hebrew 'no satan' as total peace given expressly so that the temple might be built.
Henry turns 'no adversary' into a lesson that Satan hinders temple work, so seasons of rest must be spent building.
Gill reads the rest simply as freedom from foreign enemies, Solomon having no wars.
Gill reads the purpose as building a house for God's worship, honour, and glory.
Clarke presses 'the name of the Lord' to mean God himself, so the house is built to the infinite and eternal Jehovah.
Keil & Delitzsch ground the resolve in the promise of 2 Samuel 7:12-13 that David's son would build the house.
Henry takes the promise as encouragement: assurance of success should quicken good undertakings.
Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott locate the cedar forest in Hiram's territory, note the added request for cypress and a skilled artist, and gather the Scriptures on Lebanon's timber.
Clarke, citing Vitruvius, shows that felling timber is a real art in which the Sidonians excelled Israel.
Henry contrasts Israel's husbandry with Sidonian craft and holds piety a greater gift than ingenuity, hearing Isaiah 60's Gentiles building the temple.
Gill notes that part of Lebanon belonged to Israel and part to Hiram, who supplied both trees and men to cut them.
Ellicott (with Keil & Delitzsch) reads Hiram's blessing of Jehovah as deference to Israel rather than acceptance of him as the one true God.
Clarke judges Hiram a genuine, if imperfect, worshipper of the true God.
Henry dwells on Hiram's generous joy and the blessing of godliness continued in families, warning against envy of others' gifts.
Gill marks that Hiram blessed the Lord upon hearing Solomon's letter read.

debates.question: Was Hiram a true worshipper of the God of Israel, or only diplomatically deferential?

sides.position: a genuine worshipper of the true God
his blessing is deference to Israel, not faith in Jehovah as the one true God

schools.summary: Henry makes Hiram's 'I have considered the things' a lesson in the prudence that deliberates before bargaining, citing Proverbs 31:16.
Keil & Delitzsch trace the practical arrangement to float the timber by sea-rafts to Joppa, the nearest harbour to Jerusalem.
Gill notes only that the verse introduces Hiram's letter of reply.
Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott trace the rafting of timber to Joppa and read 'food for his household' against Tyre being 'nourished' by Palestine (Acts 12:20; Ezekiel 27:17).
Clarke details the squared timber floated to Joppa or Jamnia, ready to assemble without further tools.
Gill identifies the sea as the Mediterranean, on which Tyre stood.
Henry reads the timber-for-food exchange as Providence making nations mutually dependent, to the glory of the common Father.
Henry reads the delivery as each party keeping his engagement, and draws the lesson of justice on both sides of every bargain (Deuteronomy 16:20).
Gill describes Hiram's servants felling the trees and floating them in rafts to Solomon.
Keil & Delitzsch note that Hiram supplied cedar and fir 'according to all his desire,' the whole request met.
Gill calculates the twenty thousand cors of wheat as an immense provision, near thirteen million pounds.
Clarke fills out the payment from 2 Chronicles 2:10 and notes the versions read 'twenty thousand' for the Hebrew 'twenty' measures of oil.
Ellicott judges the tiny figure for oil disproportionate and adopts the Greek 'twenty thousand baths,' the wheat agreeing with the court's daily provision (1 Kings 4:22).

debates.question: Was the annual oil 'twenty measures' or 'twenty thousand baths'?

sides.position: twenty thousand baths, with the Septuagint, versions, Chronicles, and Josephus
the received Hebrew figure of twenty measures
holders: John Gill (working with the text as it stands)

schools.summary: Gill sees Solomon's God-given wisdom displayed in his temple preparations, his agreements with Hiram, and his keeping of the friendship.
Henry ranks the wisdom above any gift of Hiram's and makes it the ground of a lasting league, urging friendship with the honest and fair.
Keil & Delitzsch extend the wisdom beyond the treaty to Solomon's wise use of his own subjects, marking the verse as a hinge to the levy.
Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott identify the levy as tribute-labour from all Israel, distinct from the Canaanite bondmen, and note its tension with 1 Kings 9:22.
Clarke stresses the merciful rota of one month's work to two months' rest under Adoniram, all the levy being Israelites.
Henry notes the Israelites had the lighter, more honourable work and were not to be overworked, since servants must rest too.
Gill clarifies that the levy was of men, not money.

debates.question: How can this levy be squared with 1 Kings 9:22, that Solomon made no Israelite bondmen?

sides.position: the Israelites bore only a light rotated corvée and oversight, not true bondage, which fell on the subject races
exceptional even so, and perhaps the seed of a later oppressive despotism

schools.summary: Keil & Delitzsch detail the monthly rotation under Adoniram and, from the verb hatsab, identify the 150,000 as stone-workers distinct from the timber levy.
Gill notes the ten thousand a month are those set to work with Hiram's men felling and squaring timber.
Henry commends the merciful rota that gave two months' rest for each month's labour.
Gill takes the seventy thousand burden-bearers as strangers who carried the quarried stones to Jerusalem.
Clarke identifies the whole mass as strangers or proselytes dwelling among Israel, per 2 Chronicles 2:17.
Keil & Delitzsch separate the 150,000 from the Israelites as the Canaanite tribute-slaves who did the heavy stone-work.
Henry notes soberly that these captive labourers, unlike the Israelites, had no resting times, being doomed to servitude.

themes: strangers in the work of God

schools.summary: Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott weigh the 3,300 overseers against the varying totals in 1 Kings 9:23 and Chronicles, and debate whether they were Israelite or drawn from the subject races.
Gill reads them as the chief officers over the whole work, chiefly the hewing and carriage of stone.
Clarke notes the parallel and the Septuagint give 3,600 rather than 3,300.

debates.question: What accounts for the differing totals of overseers, and were they Israelite or foreign?

sides.position: different classes and tiers of officer, with some textual variation, reconcile the numbers
the Chronicles' figure suggests the overseers were drawn from the subject races, like Egypt's taskmasters

schools.summary: Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott read the 'costly' stones as the vast worked substructure of the temple area on Mount Moriah, still partly visible, transported at enormous cost.
Clarke distinguishes the stones as great in size, costly in labour, and squared and polished.
Gill takes 'great' of quantity and size, strong and durable stones fit for the foundation.
Henry makes the costly hidden foundation-stones a figure of Christ the elect and precious cornerstone, urging sincerity in the unseen part of religion.
Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott recover 'Giblites' (men of Gebal/Byblos) behind the gloss 'stone-squarers,' skilled Phoenician builders working with Solomon's and Hiram's men.
Clarke prefers the margin's 'Giblites,' though the versions adopted 'stone-squarers' from the context of stone-cutting.
Gill infers that Solomon had hewers of stone as well as of wood from Hiram, timber and stone now made ready.
